content.text:
<user_query>
Help me to review the agentic runner setup in this project. We occured many issues when running in real workflows. But GitHub hosted runner works well. We need to make self-hosted works well as GitHub runner. Here is some related documents: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/guides/self-hosted-runners/
https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/runners/self-hosted-runners

How should we make our self-hosted runner works better? Should it be ran in native or in docker(current implement)? Give me some advises.
</user_query>

input.command: ls /home/an-lee/projects/gh-sr && echo "---" && ls /home/an-lee/projects/gh-sr/internal 2>/dev/null || echo "no internal dir"
input.description: List top-level files and internal directory
List runner, host, and ops directories

content.text: Now I have a thorough understanding of the codebase. Let me compile my analysis.

Based on my thorough review of the codebase, here is my analysis and recommendations.

---

## Key Findings

### What your current Docker mode does

The project runs runners inside `ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest` containers. For agentic (gh-aw) workflows, it uses a custom `AgenticRunnerDockerImage` that extends the base image with `iptables` and `docker-compose-plugin`. The container gets:
- `--restart unless-stopped` (auto-restart on daemon restart)
- The host Docker socket bind-mounted at `/var/run/docker.sock` + `--group-add <GID>`
- `--network host` and `--cap-add NET_ADMIN` for agentic profile

### Why gh-aw fails on self-hosted vs GitHub-hosted

GitHub-hosted runners run natively on a clean Linux VM. Your docker-mode self-hosted runner runs inside a container, so:

1. **Network namespace mismatch** — gh-aw's MCP gateway binds to `localhost:80` and health checks from within the runner job assume they're on the same network as the Docker daemon. With default bridge networking, `localhost` inside the container is isolated. `profile: agentic` sets `--network host` to fix this, but it must be explicitly set.

2. **iptables / DOCKER-USER chain** — gh-aw's Agent Workflow Firewall needs `iptables` inside the container and `NET_ADMIN` capability. The base `ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner` image has neither. Only `profile: agentic` triggers the custom `AgenticRunnerDockerImage` build that installs them.

3. **Docker socket permission inside the container** — The container's `runner` user (uid 1001) must reach `/var/run/docker.sock`. The code handles this with `--group-add <GID>`, but only if the socket GID probe succeeds. Failures fall back to mounting without `--group-add`, leading to `permission denied` at runtime.

4. **Ephemeral runners losing logs** — With `ephemeral: true`, containers are removed after one job. Logs go with them. GitHub docs explicitly warn about this.

5. **Runner version drift** — The image tag is pinned to `latest`, so the runner binary version inside the container is whatever was in the image when it was last pulled. Stale images cause version-mismatch failures because GitHub's service stops queueing jobs when the runner is more than 30 days out of date.

---

## Docker vs Native for gh-aw Agentic Workflows

| Factor | Docker (`profile: agentic`) | Native Linux |
|---|---|---|
| Job isolation | Each job gets the container's FS (but container persists between jobs unless ephemeral) | Runs directly on host FS |
| Host networking required | Yes (`--network host` already set by profile) | Automatic — job IS the host |
| iptables/NET_ADMIN | Must be in image + `--cap-add` (already handled by profile) | Available natively if runner user has passwordless sudo |
| Docker socket | Bind-mounted; GID dance needed | Available natively to runner user's group |
| Complexity | More moving parts (socket probe, GID, image build) | Fewer layers |
| GitHub-hosted parity | Closer: both are effectively a clean Linux env | Closest: runner is a bare process like GitHub-hosted |
| Environment staleness | Image must be re-pulled to update packages | `installdependencies.sh` runs fresh each setup |

**Recommendation: for agentic workflows, native Linux is the closest match to GitHub-hosted and has fewer failure modes.** Docker mode makes sense when you need OS isolation between jobs or are running on a non-Linux host. For a dedicated Linux VPS running gh-aw jobs, native mode removes the entire socket-permission / network-namespace / image-staleness problem class.

---

## Specific Advice per Issue Class

### 1. Missing `profile: agentic` (most common root cause)
Every runner that runs gh-aw jobs must have `profile: agentic` set. Without it:
- No `--network host` → MCP gateway health check fails
- No `NET_ADMIN` → AWF iptables setup fails
- Base image has no `iptables`

```yaml
runners:
  - name: aw-runner
    repo: owner/repo
    host: vps-1
    profile: agentic
    count: 2
```

### 2. Stale runner image
The code always uses `ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest` and only pulls on setup or when the image is absent locally. After the initial pull the image can become stale. Add `docker pull` to your maintenance cycle or use `gh sr update <runner>` which removes/re-creates the container:

```bash
gh sr update aw-runner   # removes container, re-pulls image, re-registers
```

For the agentic image (`ghcr.io/an-lee/agentic-runner:latest`), the same applies — it extends `latest` at build time, so a `docker pull ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest` on the host followed by `gh sr update` is needed to stay current.

### 3. Docker socket GID probe failures
The `stat -c '%g'` probe can fail silently (returns empty or errors) and the code falls back to mounting without `--group-add`. To make this reliable, verify from the host:

```bash
stat -c '%g' /var/run/docker.sock
# and confirm the user is in that group
id
```

If the runner user is already in the `docker` group (preferred), the probe is a safety net rather than a requirement.

### 4. Log retention for ephemeral runners
With `ephemeral: true`, containers are removed after one job and logs are gone. Before going to production with ephemeral runners, add external log shipping. The simplest approach: mount a host log directory into the container.

Currently `dockerStartCommand` does not mount a log volume. You would need to add a `-v /path/on/host/logs:/home/runner/_diag` volume to the `dockerStartOpts`/`dockerStartCommand` in `internal/runner/docker.go`.

### 5. Runner version monitoring
`gh sr doctor` does not currently check whether the runner binary version inside a container is recent enough. A stale version (>30 days behind latest) causes GitHub to stop queueing jobs with no obvious error on the runner side. Add a periodic `gh sr update` to your maintenance schedule or subscribe to [actions/runner releases](https://github.com/actions/runner/releases).

### 6. Autostart reliability
Docker containers use `--restart unless-stopped` which survives Docker daemon restarts. However, if the Docker daemon itself does not start at boot (e.g. not enabled via systemd), containers won't come back. Verify on the host:

```bash
sudo systemctl is-enabled docker
sudo systemctl is-active docker
```

For native mode, `gh sr service install --system` is required for boot survival on headless Linux servers.

---

## Summary Recommendation

For Linux VPS hosts running gh-aw agentic workflows:

- **Switch to `mode: native`** if you want the closest parity with GitHub-hosted runners and fewer Docker-related failure modes. The runner user needs passwordless `sudo` for gh-aw's AWF firewall (same requirement, but no socket/network/GID complexity).
- **Keep `mode: docker` with `profile: agentic`** if you need Linux container isolation on non-Linux hosts (Windows/macOS) or want multiple runners with different images. Ensure the image is regularly updated with `gh sr update`.
- In either case, run `gh sr doctor --strict` after any host change to catch issues before they surface in live workflows.

<user_query>
GitHub agentic worflow only works in Ubuntu VPS right? Do some research about it, and help me to make a list about how to make a VPS agentic-workflow-ready.
</user_query>

Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me write this up as a clear, well-structured checklist document.

---

## Research Summary: OS Compatibility

**gh-aw is not Ubuntu-only**, but Ubuntu (Linux) is by far the most reliable and supported platform. Here's what the official docs actually say:

- The runner must be **Linux-based** for full gh-aw support (AWF firewall uses `iptables`, which is Linux-only)
- macOS and Windows can run docker-mode runners with `profile: agentic`, but AWF then runs inside the Docker Desktop/OrbStack Linux VM — not the native host OS
- The gh-aw sandbox architecture explicitly assumes a Linux VM substrate (`iptables`, `docker` socket, `NET_ADMIN` capability)
- Non-sudo configurations (including ARC with `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`) are **explicitly unsupported**

So: **Linux (Ubuntu is easiest) is required for a native self-hosted runner. Any distro that supports Docker and iptables works, but Ubuntu 22.04+ is the de facto standard since the base runner image is Ubuntu-based.**

---

## VPS Agentic-Workflow Readiness Checklist

Here is a complete, ordered checklist based on the gh-aw architecture docs, sandbox requirements, and this project's own runner setup code:

### 1. OS & Kernel

- [ ] Linux (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS or 24.04 LTS recommended; Debian 12+ also works)
- [ ] Kernel must support `iptables` (`ip_tables` module loaded) — required by AWF
- [ ] Verify: `sudo iptables -L DOCKER-USER -n` should not error after Docker is installed

### 2. Docker Engine

- [ ] Install Docker Engine via `https://get.docker.com` (not the `snap` package — `snap`-installed Docker breaks env var passing)
- [ ] Docker daemon must be **enabled at boot**: `sudo systemctl enable --now docker`
- [ ] Docker's **default iptables integration must remain on** — do not set `"iptables": false` in `/etc/docker/daemon.json`
- [ ] Verify Docker version: `docker info --format '{{.ServerVersion}}'`
- [ ] Verify the DOCKER-USER chain exists: `sudo iptables -L DOCKER-USER -n`

### 3. Runner User & Permissions

- [ ] Create a dedicated runner user (e.g., `runner`) or use an existing non-root user
- [ ] Add the runner user to the `docker` group: `sudo usermod -aG docker runner`
- [ ] Reconnect SSH (or run `newgrp docker`) so the group membership takes effect
- [ ] Configure **passwordless sudo** for the runner user — required by AWF firewall during jobs:
  ```
  # /etc/sudoers.d/runner
  runner ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL
  ```
- [ ] Verify: `sudo -n true` exits 0 as the runner user
- [ ] Verify: `docker info` works without `sudo` as the runner user

### 4. Network & Ports

- [ ] Port **80 must be free** on the host — the MCP gateway container binds to `localhost:80`; verify with `ss -tlnp | grep :80`
- [ ] Outbound HTTPS (port 443) open to GitHub domains: `github.com`, `api.github.com`, `*.actions.githubusercontent.com`, `*.blob.core.windows.net`, `ghcr.io`
- [ ] Outbound access to Docker Hub / GHCR for image pulls: `registry-1.docker.io`, `ghcr.io`
- [ ] No firewall rule blocking the Docker bridge network's internal traffic (AWF uses `172.30.0.x` range by default)

### 5. SSH & Remote Access (for gh sr)

- [ ] SSH server running and accessible from your control machine
- [ ] Key-based auth configured in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` for the runner user
- [ ] Non-interactive SSH works: `ssh -o BatchMode=yes user@host true`
- [ ] Passwordless sudo works over SSH: `ssh -o BatchMode=yes user@host 'sudo -n true'`
- [ ] Host key accepted: connect once with plain `ssh` to add to `known_hosts`

### 6. gh sr Configuration

- [ ] Add the VPS under `hosts:` in `~/.config/gh-sr/runners.yml` with `os: linux` and correct `arch`
- [ ] Use `profile: agentic` on runner entries — this sets docker mode, `--network host`, `NET_ADMIN`, and `agentic` label automatically
- [ ] Set `count: 2` or more if you want concurrent agentic jobs
- [ ] For org-level runners, use `org:` + `group:` instead of `repo:`
- [ ] Run `gh sr doctor` and resolve all FAIL/WARN items before proceeding

### 7. Runner Setup & Verification

- [ ] Run `gh sr setup` — this pulls `ghcr.io/an-lee/agentic-runner:latest` (or builds it locally) and verifies Docker
- [ ] Run `gh sr up` — starts the runner container(s) with `--network host` and `--cap-add NET_ADMIN`
- [ ] Verify runner appears online in GitHub: Settings → Actions → Runners
- [ ] Verify the Docker socket is accessible inside the container:
  ```bash
  docker exec gh-runner-<instance> test -S /var/run/docker.sock && echo ok
  ```
- [ ] Install autostart so runners survive reboots:
  ```bash
  gh sr service install --system   # writes /etc/systemd/system/ghsr-runner-*.service
  loginctl enable-linger <runner-user>  # for user-mode systemd
  ```

### 8. Runner Image Maintenance

- [ ] Schedule periodic image refresh — the `ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest` base can become stale (GitHub stops queuing jobs after 30 days behind):
  ```bash
  gh sr update <runner-name>   # stops, re-pulls, re-registers
  ```
- [ ] Consider pinning a specific runner version tag instead of `latest` to control update timing

### 9. Optional: Ephemeral Runners (Security Hardening)

- [ ] If security isolation between jobs is required, set `ephemeral: true` in runner config
- [ ] Set up external log forwarding before enabling ephemeral mode — logs are lost when the container is removed (GitHub explicitly requires this)
- [ ] Consider a log volume mount (`-v /var/log/gh-runners:/home/runner/_diag`) — currently not built into `gh sr` but can be added via `docker_pre_setup` or a custom wrapper

### 10. Smoke Test

- [ ] Run a minimal gh-aw workflow targeting the runner's labels
- [ ] Confirm the AWF firewall initializes (check job logs for "iptables" setup lines)
- [ ] Confirm MCP gateway health check passes (`http://localhost:80` reachable from within the job)
- [ ] Run `gh sr doctor --strict` after the first successful job to confirm everything is healthy
